coredata.dc:title: A reliable ratiometric fluorescence sensing of heparin and its antidote based on cationic carbon quantum dots and acid red 87
coredata.dc:creator: Alhazzani, Khalid
A.Z., Alanazi
Alaseem, Ali M.
Al Awadh, Saeed Abdullah
Alanazi, Saleh A.
AlSayyari, Abdulaziz Abdullah
Alanazi, Mohammed M.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: A reliable, selective, and sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensor was fabricated for the detection of protamine (PROT) and heparin (HEPR). It is based on a rational combination of Acid Red 87 (AR 87) as an anionic dye and polyethyleneimine modified carbon quantum dots (PEI@CQDs) as cationic fluorescent nanoparticles via electrostatic interaction. Addition of positively charged PROT quenched the negatively charged AR 87 via static quenching; while, the addition of HEPR restored the fluorescence of AR 87 via the formation of PROT-HEPR complex. Simultaneously, the fluorescence emission of PEI@CQDs remained constant. Thus, a ratiometric response (FAR 87/FPEI@CQDs) was linear with the concentration of PROT and HEPR in the range of 0.006–20 µg mL−1 and 0.018–22 µg mL−1 with detection limits (S/N) of 1.7 ng mL−1 and 6.0 ng mL−1, respectively. The analytical sensing platform offers several advantages including good selectivity, low LOD values, low-cost, and fast-response. Importantly, the sensing platform was used to detect PROT and HEPR in human serum and urine samples with satisfactorily recoveries % and RSDs %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Protamine
Heparin
PEI@CQDs
AR 87
Electrostatic interaction
Ratiometric fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of human serum and urine samples
Ratiometric detection of PROT and HEPR

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education – Kingdom of Saudi Arabi

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deputyship for Research & Innovation, “Ministry of Education” in Saudi Arabia for funding this research work through the project no. (IFKSURG-193)”.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of PEI@CQDs (Inset is size distribution diagram); (B) UV/Vis and fluorescence spectra of PEI@GQDs; (C) EDX of PEI@CQDs; (D) PXRD of PEI@CQDs; (E) FTIR spectra of (a) CQDs, (b) PEI, and (c) PEI@CQDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PEI@CQDs, AR 87, and PEI@CQDs/AR 87; (B) Zeta-potentials of PEI@CQDs, AR 87, and PEI@CQDs/AR 87; (C) Fluorescence spectra of PEI@CQDs/AR 87, PEI@CQDs/AR 87/PROT, and PEI@CQDs/AR 87/PROT/HEPR; (D) Stern-Volmer plots at different temperatures; (E) Fluorescence lifetimes of PEI@CQDs/AR 87 before and after addition of PROT; (F) UV/Vis absorption spectra of AR 87 before and after addition of PROT.
(A) The effect of different PROT concentrations on the fluorescence emission of AR 87/PEI@CQDs. (B) The effect of different HEPR concentrations on the fluorescence emission of AR 87/PEI@CQDs /PROT.
Effect of different interfering species (5.0 µg mL−1) on the fluorescence emission of AR 87/PEI@CQDs (A) and AR 87/PEI@CQDs/PROT.
Graphical representation for the detection of PROT and HEPR using PEI@CQDs.
Comparison between the quantitative parameters of the proposed sensor and other reported sensors for the determination of PROT and HEPR.
Application of AR 87/PEI@CQDs sensing platform for the determination of PROT.
Application of AR 87/PEI@CQDs/PROT sensing platform for the determination of HEPR.

head.ce:title: A reliable ratiometric fluorescence sensing of heparin and its antidote based on cationic carbon quantum dots and acid red 87

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A reliable, selective, and sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensor was fabricated for the detection of protamine (PROT) and heparin (HEPR). It is based on a rational combination of Acid Red 87 (AR 87) as an anionic dye and polyethyleneimine modified carbon quantum dots (PEI@CQDs) as cationic fluorescent nanoparticles via electrostatic interaction. Addition of positively charged PROT quenched the negatively charged AR 87 via static quenching; while, the addition of HEPR restored the fluorescence of AR 87 via the formation of PROT-HEPR complex. Simultaneously, the fluorescence emission of PEI@CQDs remained constant. Thus, a ratiometric response (FAR 87/FPEI@CQDs) was linear with the concentration of PROT and HEPR in the range of 0.006–20 µg mL−1 and 0.018–22 µg mL−1 with detection limits (S/N) of 1.7 ng mL−1 and 6.0 ng mL−1, respectively. The analytical sensing platform offers several advantages including good selectivity, low LOD values, low-cost, and fast-response. Importantly, the sensing platform was used to detect PROT and HEPR in human serum and urine samples with satisfactorily recoveries % and RSDs %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Protamine (PROT), a poly cationic protein, carries more than twenty positive charges under physiological conditions. It is used as antidote for the treatment of heparin overdose via electrostatic interaction [1,2]. Moreover, it can directly detect the influences of nucleases on the oxidative damage and DNA cleavage [3,4]. It is reported that excessive amount of PROT causes serious side-effects including bradycardia, dyspnea, flushing, pulmonary arterial hypertension, and sudden decrease in the blood pressure [5,6]. Heparin (HEPR), a highly sulfonated glycosaminoglycan, is widely used as anti-coagulant agent during surgical procedures. It plays a crucial role in many pathophysiological processes such as lipid transport, blood circulation, cell growth, metabolism, and venous thromboembolism [7,8]. It is necessary to accurately determine HEPR as a result of its short biological t½ and potential bleeding risk [9,10]. PROT and HEPR were determined using various analytical techniques including HPLC [11,12], fluorometry [13,14], and colorimetry [15,16]. Although most of the above-mentioned methods are effective for the determination of PROT and HEPR; but, it is important to develop much simpler analytical sensors for their determination especially during bio-analysis. Fluorescence detection method is one of the most promising strategies for the detection of various analytes [17–19]. Fluorometric-based single-signal analyses suffer from many limitations including the interference from pretreatment steps, instrumentation, surrounding environment, and back-ground signals. In contrast, fluorescent sensors involving dual-emission signals can overcome these limitations and retain the accuracy of the output response [20].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have several advantages including hydrophilic nature, ease-of-dispersion, ultrasmall, photoluminescence properties, ease-of-functionalization, eco-friendly, and good stability. These properties enabled CQDs to be used in different applications including drug delivery, sensing, cell-imaging, etc. [21–27]. Polyethyleneimine (PEI), a poly-cationic passivator, is widely used for surface modification of CQDs [28]. It is reported that PEI enhances the photoluminescence and stability of CQDs [29].

Herein, a selective and sensitive dual-signal platform was proposed for the ratiometric determination of PROT and HEPR. The sensor is based on a ratiometric mixing of PEI-modified CQDs and acid red 87 (AR 87) via electrostatic interaction. Addition of PROT (positively charged poly-cation) quenched the fluorescence emission of AR 87 (negatively-charged dye) based on the electrostatic interaction. When HEPR was introduced, it bound the positively charged PROT, resulting in the recovery of the fluorescence emission of AR 87 at 545 nm. Simultaneously, the emission of PEI@CQDs at 470 nm remained constant. As a result, the ratiometric response (F545/F470) could be used to quantitatively estimate both PROT and HEPR.

Protamine (PROT, > 90 %), heparin (HEPR, > 96 %), chodriotin sulfate (CHS, ≥98 %), cysteine (CYS, ≥ 97 %), dopamine (DA, ≥97 %), glutathione (GLUT, ≥97 %), uric acid (UA, ≥97 %), polyethyleneimine (PEI, >98 %), glycine (GLY, ≥97 %), lysine (LYS, ≥98 %), ascorbic acid (AA, ≥98 %), and Tris-HCl buffer were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany. Citric acid (CA, ≥97 %), glucose (GLU, ≥97 %), and acid red 87 (AR 87, AR) were purchased from Merck.
Details for instrumentation were listed in Electronic Supplementary Material (ESM).

Into a beaker, 1.5 g CA was mixed with 40 mL of 2.5 mg mL−1 PEI and sonicated for 10 min. Then, the resultant mixture was heated at 185 °C for 5 h. The final product was centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 20 min before dialyzing (molecular weight = 3000 Da) of the final solution. The quantum yield of PEI@CQDs was calculated against quinine sulfate reference solution and was calculated as 28.4 %.

Different volumes of PROT (25 mg mL−1) were mixed with 150 µL Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.0) and 100 µL of 0.05 mM AR 87. The resultant solution was let to stand at room temperature for 2 min. Then, 50 µL of 2.0 mg mL−1 of PEI@CQDs was added before the volume was completed to 1 mL with double distilled water (DDW). The photoluminescence spectra were recorded after excitation at 360 nm (Scheme 1 ).

150 µL of PROT (25 mg mL−1) was mixed with 150 µL Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.0) and 100 µL of 0.05 mM AR 87. The resultant solution was let to stand at room temperature for 2 min. Then, 50 µL of 2.0 mg mL−1 of PEI@CQDs was added, followed by different volumes of 10 mg mL−1 HEPR. The resultant mixed solutions were incubated at room temperature for 3 min. Finally, the volume was completed to 1 mL with DDW before recording the spectra after excitation at 360 nm (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of human serum and urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum and urine samples were prepared according to our previous reports [30–32]. Details of preparation of biological samples were listed in ESM.

The sensing interface was characterized using different techniques including TEM, UV/Vis, fluorescence spectroscopy, EDX, PXRD, and XPS. Fig. 1 A shows the TEM image of well-dispersed PEI@CQDs with an average size of 3.4 nm. Fig. 1B exhibits UV/Vis spectrum of PEI@CQDs with two absorption maxima at 265 nm and 340 nm, corresponding to π-π* of C=C and n-π* of C=O transitions, respectively [33]. Moreover, the fluorescence spectroscopy exhibited that the as-fabricated PEI@CQDs showed an emission spectrum at 470 nm after excitation at 360 nm. Fig. 1C explores the EDX spectrum of PEI@CQDs hybrid with characteristic peaks of C, O, and N. The PXRD of PEI@CQDs was shown in Fig. 1D with diffraction peaks at 26.36° and 42.70°, which are attributed to (002) and (101) diffraction angles of nanocarbon, respectively [28]. Moreover, FTIR technique was used to show the difference between CQDs, PEI, and PEI@CQDs. Fig. 1E (a) exhibits the FTIR spectrum of CQDs where it shows absorption bands at 3415 cm−1, 1708 cm−1, and 1630 cm−1, corresponding to υ (OH),υ (C=O), and δ (OH), respectively [34,35]. Fig. 1E (b) shows the FTIR spectrum of PEI where it exhibits absorption bands at 3375 cm−1, 1620 cm−1, and 1317 cm−1, which refer to υ (NH), δ (NH), and υ (C-N), respectively [33]. Fig. 1E (c) shows the FTIR spectrum of PEI@CQDs. XPS technique was used to explore the surface functional groups of PEI@CQDs. Fig.S1(a) shows the full spectrum of PEI@CQDs with three identifiable peaks of C, O, and N. Fig.S1(b) exhibits the HR-XPS spectra of C1 s with well-seen peaks at 283.5 eV, 284.7 eV, and 285.6 eV, which are assigned to C-C/C=C, C-N, and C=N/C=O, respectively. Fig.S1(c) shows the HR-XPS spectra of O1 s with three peaks at 528.4 eV, 529.5 eV, and 530.6 eV, which are assigned to C-O, C=O, and C-O-C/C-OH, respectively. Fig.S1(d) reveals the HR-XPS of N1 s with tow peaks at 396.6 eV and 398.4 eV, referring to the pyridinic and pyrrolic N, respectively [36,37]. It was found that the emission wavelength was varied with the excitation wavelength in the range of 320–390 nm. Fig.S2 shows that the maximum emission intensity was obtained at 470 nm after excitation at 360 nm. In addition, the stability of the as-synthesized PEI@CQDs was monitored under various conditions including concentration of salt, pH, temperature, and irradiation time. It was shown that the PEI@CQDs were stable under high-concentration of NaCl (3.0 M), wide-range of pH values (5–10), and long irradiation time (40 min). Increasing the temperature of micro-environment decreased the fluorescence emission of PEI@CQDs (Fig.S3). Thus, a temperature of 25 °C (room temperature) was used to fabricate sensors based on CQDs [38].

Fig. 2 A shows the fluorescence emissions of PEI@CQDs and AR 87 are positioned at 470 nm and 545 nm, respectively. Moreover, Zeta-potentials of PEI@CQDs, AR 87, and PEI@CQDs/AR 87 are −13.2 mv, 5.23, and −4.34 mv, respectively, confirming that the interaction between PEI@CQDs and AR 87 was electrostatic interaction (Fig. 2B). Addition of PROT, a positively charged peptide, combines with AR 87 leading to the formation of non-fluorescent complex [39]. Thus, the fluorescence emission of AR 87 at 545 nm was decreased while the fluorescence emission of PEI@CQDs was not affected. After addition of HEPR, a negatively charged poly-anion, it specifically combines PROT, recovering the fluorescence of AR 87 (Fig. 2C). The mechanism of the interaction between AR 87 and PROT was investigated on the basis of Stern-Volmer equation [40]: F°/F = 1 + KSV Q where F° and F are fluorescence emission of AR 87 before and after addition of AR 87, KSV is Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and Q is the PROT concentration. Fig. 2D shows that the Stern-Volmer plots under different temperatures (293 K, 313 K, and 333 K) are in a good linearity. It was found that the KSV (quenching constant) at 333 K, 313 K, and 293 K were 0.68 × 103 g−1 L, 0.78 × 103 g−1 L, and 0.80 × 103 g−1 L, respectively, revealing that the KSV values were decreased with increasing the temperature value, referring to static-quenching [41]. In addition, the fluorescence lifetimes of AR 87 before and after addition of PROT were measured (Fig. 2E). It was found that the fluorescence lifetimes were not appreciably changed, proving that the static quenching occurred between AR 87 and PROT [42,43]. The UV/Vis absorption spectra of AR 87 were recorded before and after addition of PROT. It was found that a new absorption band was observed after addition of PROT, referring to a formation of new interaction product between AR 87 and PROT (Fig. 2F). In addition, the AR 87 exhibits FT-IR bands at 3360 cm−1, 1715 cm−1, and 1625 cm−1, which are assigned to υ (OH), υ (C=O), and δ (OH), respectively. Upon interaction with PROT, new bands were appeared at 2830–2900 cm−1, 1650 cm−1, and 1280 cm−1, which are attributed to υ (CH2), δ (NH), and υ (C-N), respectively of the arginine moiety in PROT (Fig. S4). Moreover, the XPS technique was used to verify the interaction between AR 87 and PROT (Fig. S5). It was found that the XPS of AR 87 has C 1s and O 1s peaks, while the XPS of AR 87/PROT shows additional peak of N 1s from arginine moiety of PROT. Moreover, the peaks in the XPS spectrum of AR 87/PROT were slightly shifted as a result of the interaction between COOH group of AR 87 and NH2 group of arginine moiety of PROT.

Important parameters of the entire analytical system should be optimized to improve the sensitivity of the detection. Therefore, variables affecting detection of PROT and HEPR were optimized including pH and reaction time. For PROT detection, it was found that the detection system was not significantly affected by the variation of pH values, suggesting the stability of the detection system in the pH range of 6.5–9.0 (Fig. S6A). Therefore, physiological pH (7.0) was chosen as an optimum value for detection of PROT. Moreover, the effect of the reaction time was also studied during the detection of PROT (Fig. S6B). It can be seen that the ratio response (FAR 87/FPEI@CQDs) was decreased until reaching the constant value at 2 min. Therefore, reaction time of 2 min was selected as an optimum reaction time for PROT detection. For HEPR detection, it was found that the response ratio (FAR 87/FPEI@CQDs) was not affected by variation of pH values (Fig. S6C), while the ratio was increased with a reaction time until constant value at 3 min. As a result, 3 min was selected as an optimum reaction time for determination of HEPR (Fig. S6D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection of PROT and HEPR
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 A shows that the ratiometric responses (FAR 87/FPEI@CQDs) were decreased after addition of different PROT concentrations. Therefore, a linear plot was obtained between FAR 87/FPEI@CQDs and PROT concentration in the range of 0.006–20 µg mL−1 with a linear regression of FAR 87/FPEI@CQDs = 0.02 + 1.756 CPROT (R2 = 0.9982) and detection limit (S/N = 3) of 1.7 ng mL−1. Interestingly, the addition of HEPR resulted in the enhancement of PEI@CQDs fluorescence emission due to complexation with PROT. Therefore, addition of different HEPR concentrations resulted in increasing the FAR 87/FPEI@CQDs response (Fig. 3B). A linear plot was obtained between FAR 87/FPEI@CQDs and HEPR concentration in the range of 0.018–22 µg mL−1 with a regression equation of FAR 87/FPEI@CQDs = 0.015 + 0.544 CHEPR (R2 = 0.9988) and detection limit (S/N = 3) of 6.0 ng mL−1. Table 1 summarizes the analytical parameters of the proposed sensing system and other reported systems for estimation of PROT and HEPR. It was concluded that the sensing system had widest linear-dynamic range and low LOD value. Therefore, our analytical detection system can be used efficiently for accurate and sensitive detection of PROT and HEPR.

The influence of different interfering species on the ratiometric response of AR 87/PEI@CQDs towards PROT and HEPR detection was explored. Fig. 4 revealed that 5.0 µg mL−1 of interfering species did not significantly alter the ratiometric response of 8.0 µg mL−1 PROT and 7.0 µg mL−1 HEPR. These results confirmed that the sensing interface had good anti-interference capability for estimation of PROT and HEPR in biological samples.

The stability of AR 87/PEI@CQDs was monitored over 50 days by measuring their relative activity % (RA %). It was found that the AR 87/PEI@CQDs sensor restored its activity during this period without a significant change in RA %, confirming the long-term stability of the AR 87/PEI@CQDs. Moreover, the reusability of the sensor is feasible since the AR 87/PEI@CQDs sensor can be switched on by simple filtration at least three successive cycles. Fig. S7 explores the profile of fluorescence responses of AR 87/PEI@CQDs during sequential cycles, which is less than 4 % loss of fluorescence in the third cycle.

In order to evaluate the potential application of the proposed sensing platform, it was utilized to detect PROT and HEPR in human serum and urine samples. The results were concluded in Tables 2 and Table 3 . No PROT and HEPR were detected in biological samples, while various concentrations of PROT and HEPR were spiked into the samples for analysis. The recoveries % were laid in the range of 96.5–104.0 %, suggesting the robustness and reliability of the proposed sensing system for determination of PROT and HEPR in real samples. Moreover, it was noted that the results of the proposed system did not significantly differ from the results obtained from a reported method [13], referring to the accuracy of the proposed sensing platform.

Herein, a reliable ratiometric fluorescence platform was proposed for sensitive and selective detection of PROT and HEPR. The sensing platform is based on a combination of positively charged PEI@CQDs and negatively charged anionic dye AR 87 (AR 87/PEI@CQDs). Addition of PROT to AR 87/PEI@CQDs system resulted in the quenching of the fluorescence emission of AR 87. Moreover, addition of HEPR to AR 87/PEI@CQDs/PROT restored the fluorescence emission of AR 87 as a result of PROT-HEPR complexation. Simultaneously, the fluorescence emission of PEI@CQDs was remained unchanged. Thus, the ratiometric response (FAR 87/F PEI@CQDs) had a good linear relationship with concentrations of PROT and HEPR. The proposed sensing platform has been applied for the quantitation of PROT and HEPR in human serum and urine samples with acceptable results.

Khalid Alhazzani: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. A.Z. Alanazi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ali M. Alaseem: Investigation, Software, Validation, Visualization. Saeed Abdullah Al Awadh: Investigation, Software, Validation, Visualization. Saleh A. Alanazi: Investigation, Software, Validation, Visualization. Abdulaziz Abdullah AlSayyari: Resources. Mohammed M. Alanazi: Resources. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deputyship for Research & Innovation, “Ministry of Education” in Saudi Arabia for funding this research work through the project no. (IFKSURG-193)”.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108666.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
